Q1: I tried to keep a consistent routine, honestly. I would set aside time on weekday evenings, usually around 16 hours spread across the week, to go through my readings and written materials. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time making notes and rewriting concepts in my own words. The routine helped me feel in control even when things got overwhelming with all 16 courses I was juggling.
Q2: I really tried to stick to regular times because that's what keeps me grounded. But with so many online courses, sometimes deadlines would pile up and I'd have to shift things around. When that happened, I felt thrown off and a bit stressed, but I'd always try to get back to my normal schedule as soon as possible. Consistency is important to me even if I can't always achieve it perfectly.
Q3: Usually I logged in because I knew I had to check something—an announcement, a deadline, or to participate in discussions since I know that matters. Sometimes I'd log in just out of habit, like it became part of my evening routine. The peer support group I'm in also reminded me to stay active, so their messages would prompt me to check in more often than I might have otherwise.
Q4: Yes, definitely. After the first few weeks, logging into the LMS just became something I did automatically in the evenings. I'd make my tea, sit down, and open the platform before even thinking about it. The peer support connections helped because we'd sometimes coordinate when to be online together, which made it feel more like a social habit than a chore.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me because that's how I learn best. I also found the discussion forums surprisingly helpful—posting there 19 times throughout the course helped me process ideas by writing them out. The peer interactions, about 23 of them, gave me different perspectives that I wouldn't have gotten just studying alone.
Q6: I'll admit I didn't watch as many videos as I probably should have, even though I logged quite a bit of video time. Sometimes I'd have them playing but I wasn't fully absorbing them because reading works better for me. I also delayed some assignments more than I should have—my completion rate wasn't great at around 27%, which I'm not proud of, but when I did submit, I put real effort in.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to what I needed to know for exams or when it helped me understand something I was confused about. The discussion posts felt worthwhile because I was engaging with real people and getting feedback. If something felt like busywork without a clear purpose, I struggled to prioritize it over other courses.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford with limited resources and so many courses. Required ones I would do no matter what because they affected my grade. But sometimes optional things, like extra readings or peer discussions, ended up being more valuable for actual learning. It's just that when you're stretched thin, you have to make hard choices about where to put your energy.
Q9: Oh, absolutely. I downloaded almost everything I could and studied from printed notes or saved PDFs on my phone. With limited resources, I couldn't always rely on having stable internet, so offline studying became essential. I'd spend hours with my handwritten notes, rewriting and reorganizing information in ways that wouldn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were many evenings where I sat with my printed materials and notebooks, going over concepts for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd also study with peers in person or over messaging apps, quizzing each other and explaining things, which was really valuable learning but completely invisible online. My exam score of 63.74 reflects learning that happened mostly offline.
Q11: Yes, this was my main strategy actually. I'd access a reading or resource once, download it or copy the important parts into my notes, and then never open it again on the platform. It was more efficient for me to work with physical or saved materials than to keep logging in repeatedly. The LMS was more like a library where I'd check things out rather than a place where I'd stay and study.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, especially when multiple deadlines from different courses hit at once. My attendance dropped quite a bit—only about 38%—because I was overwhelmed trying to manage everything. I felt surprised sometimes by how quickly things piled up. Those weeks I'd feel my stress spike and I'd have to just focus on surviving rather than thriving.
Q13: Definitely. Fatigue was a big factor—high motivation doesn't mean unlimited energy, and sometimes I was just exhausted. Competing responsibilities from other courses meant I couldn't give this one as much attention as I wanted. When I was confused about something, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS because I didn't want to face material I didn't understand. It was easier to work with what I already had downloaded.
Q14: Yes, honestly quite often. I'd have videos playing while doing other things, or I'd scroll through materials without really processing them. Sometimes I logged in just to feel like I was doing something, but my mind was elsewhere. Those sessions counted as activity but weren't real engagement—I was going through motions without the mental energy to actually learn.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, which is why my completion rate was so low. Even when I knew they were important, I'd tell myself I'd do them later because I was tired or had other urgent things. The peer support helped push me sometimes, but other times I just couldn't bring myself to start. When I did complete assignments though, I scored well—93.14—so it wasn't about ability, just about getting started.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main driver of when I actually submitted things. I'd know about assignments for weeks but only really focus on them when the deadline was close. The LMS would probably show spikes in my activity right before due dates. It's not ideal, but with so many courses, I had to triage constantly and deadlines told me what was most urgent.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, but sometimes in complicated ways. Good feedback motivated me to keep going and made me feel like my effort was recognized. But when feedback was delayed or unclear, I felt disconnected from the course. The peer support was actually more immediate—getting reactions from classmates in discussions felt more motivating than waiting for official feedback.
Q18: The structure gave me a framework to follow, which I needed. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me plan my routine, even if I didn't always follow through perfectly. But with 16 online courses, sometimes the structures of different courses would conflict, and I'd have to choose which one to prioritize. A more flexible structure might have helped me manage better.
Q19: The discussion forums and peer interaction features made engagement easier because I could connect with others and feel less alone in the struggle. What made it harder was when there was too much content to get through or when instructions weren't clear. Also, not having great resources meant I couldn't always access things when I needed to, which created gaps in my participation.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I was highly engaged in terms of logging in, watching videos, posting in discussions—but my assignment completion was low. For me, it was about being overwhelmed and having to prioritize. I was learning and participating, but the actual submission of work suffered. It doesn't mean the student isn't trying; sometimes life just makes it hard to finish everything.
Q21: That makes sense to me too. If someone studies mostly offline like I do, their LMS activity would be low but they could still learn the material well. I downloaded things and studied from notes, so my actual learning time was much higher than what the system recorded. Performance on exams can come from deep offline engagement that the platform never sees.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, how often, what you click on. They can track participation in discussions and assignment submissions. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline studying, the emotional state of the student. They can't see that I was stressed but pushing through, or that I learned more from my handwritten notes than from any video I watched.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and making an effort to learn, even when it's hard. It's not just about clicking through things or meeting minimum requirements. For me, engagement includes the mental work I do away from the screen, the discussions with peers, and the genuine curiosity I feel when something finally clicks. It's more internal than any log can capture.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with having limited resources, or how the peer support intervention genuinely helped me stay connected when I felt like giving up. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed notes, or how surprised I sometimes felt when I actually understood difficult concepts. My low mood after everything and my resilience being tested—none of that shows up in login data, but it shaped my entire experience.
